content.text: IC Handoff and De-Escalation Procedures

A long-running incident outlasts any single person's sustained focus. After four to six hours under a bridge, cognitive performance degrades in predictable ways: the IC starts over-indexing on the last piece of information they received, misses escalation timings, and makes slower delegation decisions. A clean handoff between an outgoing and an incoming IC preserves momentum without losing state.

De-escalation is the symmetric problem: knowing when it is safe to lower severity and eventually close the incident. A premature de-escalation that proves wrong costs more than leaving the incident open an extra hour. The procedures below give the IC objective criteria for both transitions.

attrs.alt: IC lifecycle diagram: Active Incident -> Handoff Window -> Incoming IC Active -> De-Escalation Check, with YES path to Closed and NO looping back through de-escalation

content.text: When to Hand Off
Handoff timing is driven by elapsed time on-bridge and cognitive load, not by incident status. The IC should initiate a handoff when any of the following conditions are met:
4-hour threshold:
The IC has been actively running the incident for four or more hours. This is the hard default trigger regardless of whether the incident is progressing.
Cognitive impairment signal:
The IC notices they are asking the same question twice, losing track of task ownership, or responding more slowly to new information.
Shift boundary:
The incident will extend past the IC's normal on-call shift end time. Do not carry the incident past a natural shift boundary without a planned handoff.
Domain expertise gap:
A deeper investigation phase requires expertise the current IC does not have (for example, a model-serving issue that needs a model infrastructure specialist to lead).

The IC must not wait for the incident to stabilise before initiating handoff. If the handoff trigger is met while the incident is volatile, the incoming IC takes over a volatile incident — that is expected and preferable to an exhausted outgoing IC remaining on point.
The Five-Minute Handoff Protocol

The handoff is a synchronous five-minute verbal briefing supplemented by a written document. Both are required. A verbal-only handoff risks the incoming IC missing context that the outgoing IC assumed was obvious. A written-only handoff lacks the two-way confirmation that the incoming IC actually understood.
Verbal Briefing Structure
The outgoing IC runs through the following in strict order. Each item should take 30 to 60 seconds:
Current status:
One sentence on where the incident stands right now. Is it contained, progressing, unknown?
Active mitigation:
What is in flight. Who is doing what. Expected completion time if known.
Unknowns and open questions:
What the team has not yet investigated. What the IC is most worried about.
Key contacts:
Who has been involved. Who is the escalation path if the incident moves to P0.
One watch item:
The single most important thing the incoming IC should watch in the next 30 minutes.
Handoff Document
The handoff document is pinned to the incident Slack channel before the verbal briefing starts. It serves as the record of state at the moment of transition.

## IC Handoff -- {incident_id}

**Outgoing IC:** @{outgoing_ic}
**Incoming IC:** @{incoming_ic}
**Handoff time:** {timestamp}
**Current severity:** P{n}

### Current Status
{one_paragraph_status}

### Active Actions
| Owner        | Action                        | ETA   |
|--------------|-------------------------------|-------|
| @tech-lead   | Rollback model version to v4  | 14:30 |
| @sre-oncall  | Drain traffic from us-east-1  | 14:20 |

### Open Questions
- [ ] Why did quality score drop only on streaming requests?
- [ ] Are eu-west-1 users affected or only us-east-1?

### Timeline (last 2 hours)
- 12:00 -- Alert fired, P2 declared
- 12:18 -- Quality drop confirmed on claude-3-5-haiku
- 13:05 -- Rollback initiated

### Escalation Path
P1: @vp-engineering (pager), P0: @ceo-on-call (phone)
Bot Command for Handoff
The incident bot generates the handoff document template pre-filled from incident metadata. The outgoing IC fills in the narrative sections before running the verbal briefing.

# In the incident Slack channel:
/ic handoff @incoming-ic-username

# Bot responds with a pre-filled template:
# - Incident ID and current severity auto-populated
# - Timeline pulled from channel message history
# - Action queue pulled from open bot tasks
# - Outgoing IC prompted to fill status, open questions, and watch item
Confirming the Handoff

Handoff is not complete until the incoming IC explicitly accepts it. This prevents the silent handoff failure mode where the outgoing IC drops off the bridge before the incoming IC has confirmed they are ready.

# Outgoing IC runs the verbal briefing, then posts:
/ic transfer-ready

# Incoming IC reviews the document and confirms in the channel:
/ic accept

# Bot records the acceptance timestamp and notifies stakeholders:
# "IC transferred from @alice to @bob at 14:22. @alice remains available
#  on standby for 30 minutes."

The outgoing IC remains available on standby for 30 minutes after the handoff acceptance. During standby the outgoing IC does not take action but is reachable if the incoming IC needs clarification. The 30-minute window is firm; after it expires the outgoing IC is off the incident.
De-Escalation Gates

De-escalation is a downward severity transition or incident closure. It requires that all active gates for the current severity tier are met. Gates are checked at fixed intervals after mitigation is declared, not continuously.
Gate Definitions by Severity
Current Severity
De-Escalation Target
Required Gates
Observation Window
P0
P1 or Closed
Safety/privacy signal cleared by on-call safety lead; error rate back to baseline; no open legal hold
30 min sustained
P1
P2 or Closed
Error rate < 1%; p99 latency <= SLA; no enterprise SLA breach pending
45 min sustained
P2
P3 or Closed
Quality score within 5% of baseline; no new customer reports in 30 min
60 min sustained
P3
Closed
No customer reports; metrics nominal; root cause identified
2 hours sustained

The observation window starts from the moment the IC declares "mitigation in place". If any gate fails before the window expires, the clock resets. A new mitigation declaration is required before the window restarts.
De-Escalation Gate Check Code
Partial De-Escalation vs Full Closure

An incident can step down through severities before closing. Skipping severity levels (for example, jumping from P1 directly to closed) is only permitted when all gates at the intermediate level are also satisfied, which the IC must document explicitly.
Partial de-escalation:
Severity decreases by one level. Pagerduty routing and communication cadence update automatically. On-call stakeholder list shrinks to match the new severity.
Full closure:
All gates are met. IC runs the close command. Bot posts a closure notification in the incident channel and the status-page update goes to resolved.
Monitoring window:

After closure, the IC sets a 24-hour monitoring alert on the primary metric that triggered the incident. If the alert fires within the window, the incident reopens automatically at the original severity.

# Step down from P1 to P2 (partial de-escalation):
/ic deescalate --to p2 --reason "Error rate below 1% for 45 min; no SLA breach"

# Close the incident:
/ic close --reason "Quality score nominal for 60 min; root cause identified: model config flag"

# Set post-closure monitoring:
/ic monitor --metric quality_score_drop_pct --threshold 5.0 --window 24h
Common Handoff Failure Modes
Failure Mode
Cause
Prevention
Context loss
Verbal-only handoff; outgoing IC summarises from memory under fatigue
Require written handoff document before verbal briefing starts
Double ownership
Both ICs taking actions simultaneously before acceptance confirmed
Bot enforces single-active-IC state; second IC is read-only until acceptance
Premature handoff
Outgoing IC initiates handoff during an active escalation, not a stable hold
Block handoff command if a P0/P1 bridge call is in progress; require explicit override
Ghost standby
Outgoing IC disconnects before 30-minute standby window expires
Bot sends 10-min standby-expiry reminder; pings outgoing IC to confirm still reachable
Premature de-escalation
IC declares mitigation before root cause confirmed; metrics rebound
Enforce observation window with automated gate check before de-escalation command succeeds
Orphaned monitoring
Post-closure monitoring alert not set; regression recurs silently
Block incident close command unless /ic monitor command has been issued
Handoff in Multi-IC Incidents

When two incidents run simultaneously and a single IC is covering both (the simultaneous-P0 scenario from the roster document), handoff must address each incident independently. The incoming IC receives two separate handoff documents, one per incident, and confirms acceptance for each before the outgoing IC stands down.

If the incoming IC's bandwidth does not cover both incidents, the second incident must escalate its own IC from the roster before the handoff can complete. The outgoing IC must not accept a handoff completion acknowledgement for any incident they cannot personally confirm is IC-covered.
Multi-Incident Handoff Checklist
Confirm roster has an available IC for each concurrent incident.
Run /ic handoff separately in each incident channel.
Incoming IC confirms acceptance in each channel before outgoing IC proceeds.
Document in each handoff which incidents the incoming IC is now holding.
Outgoing IC enters standby for each incident independently; standby windows may overlap.
Automating Handoff Quality Checks

Manual handoff quality varies with the outgoing IC's fatigue and communication skill. An automated checker can flag structural gaps in the handoff document before the verbal briefing starts, giving the outgoing IC a chance to fill in missing sections while still alert.
Handoff Document Validator

import re
from dataclasses import dataclass

REQUIRED_SECTIONS = [
    "Current Status",
    "Active Actions",
    "Open Questions",
    "Timeline",
    "Escalation Path",
]

@dataclass
class HandoffValidationResult:
    passed: bool
    missing_sections: list[str]
    warnings: list[str]


def validate_handoff_doc(markdown_text: str) -> HandoffValidationResult:
    """Check that all required sections are present and non-empty."""
    missing = []
    warnings = []

    for section in REQUIRED_SECTIONS:
        pattern = rf"^###?\s+{re.escape(section)}"
        match = re.search(pattern, markdown_text, re.MULTILINE)
        if not match:
            missing.append(section)
            continue
        # Check section is not empty (has at least one non-header line after it)
        section_start = match.end()
        next_header = re.search(r"^#{1,3}\s+", markdown_text[section_start:], re.MULTILINE)
        body = (
            markdown_text[section_start: section_start + next_header.start()]
            if next_header else markdown_text[section_start:]
        )
        if len(body.strip()) < 10:
            warnings.append(f"Section '{section}' appears empty")

    # Check action table has at least one row
    if re.search(r"\|\s*---", markdown_text):
        rows = re.findall(r"^\|[^-
][^
]+\|$", markdown_text, re.MULTILINE)
        if len(rows) < 2:  # header row + at least one data row
            warnings.append("Active Actions table appears empty")

    return HandoffValidationResult(
        passed=len(missing) == 0,
        missing_sections=missing,
        warnings=warnings,
    )


def enforce_handoff(channel_id: str, doc_text: str) -> bool:
    """
    Return True if the handoff command should be allowed to proceed.
    Posts feedback to the incident channel if validation fails.
    """
    result = validate_handoff_doc(doc_text)
    if not result.passed:
        feedback = "Handoff blocked -- missing sections: " + ", ".join(result.missing_sections)
        post_to_channel(channel_id, feedback)  # noqa: F821
        return False
    for w in result.warnings:
        post_to_channel(channel_id, f"Handoff warning: {w}")  # noqa: F821
    return True
Runbook: Handling a Refused Handoff
When the bot blocks a handoff command, the outgoing IC must fill the missing sections before retrying. Common reasons for refusal and their fixes:
Bot Message
Root Cause
Fix
Missing section: Open Questions
IC marked all questions resolved but none were documented
Add a one-line entry for every unresolved question, even tentative ones
Missing section: Escalation Path
Incident opened before P0 escalation path was set
Run /ic set-escalation before retrying handoff
Active Actions table appears empty
Tasks were tracked verbally, not in the bot
Run /ic task add for each in-flight action, then retry
Observation window not complete
IC issued /ic deescalate before window expired
Wait for the bot confirmation that the window has closed
IC Handoff Metrics
The IC programme tracks handoff quality through automated metrics collected by the incident bot. These metrics surface patterns that individual postmortems miss.
Mean time to handoff document (MTHD):
Time from handoff initiation to document posted. Target < 5 minutes. Sustained > 10 minutes indicates the template is too complex.
Handoff refusal rate:
Fraction of handoff attempts blocked by the validator. A rate above 20% suggests outgoing ICs are not maintaining the handoff document during the incident.
Post-handoff reopen rate:
Fraction of incidents that required the outgoing IC to rejoin within their 30-minute standby window. Above 15% indicates insufficient context transfer.
False de-escalation rate:
Fraction of severity decreases that were reversed within two hours. Above 10% indicates the observation window thresholds are too short for this type of incident.
Monitoring alert fire rate:
Fraction of closed incidents whose 24-hour post-closure monitoring alert fires. Above 5% per incident type is a signal that closure gates for that type need tightening.

Programme leads review these metrics quarterly alongside postmortem analysis. A rising handoff refusal rate with stable incident frequency suggests a training gap, not a process gap. A rising false de-escalation rate with no training change suggests the observation windows need lengthening for a specific incident category.
Summary

IC handoff is a synchronous five-minute verbal briefing plus a written document that captures current status, active actions, open questions, and one watch item. The incoming IC does not hold the bridge until they have explicitly accepted. The outgoing IC remains on 30-minute standby after acceptance. De-escalation requires sustained metric recovery for a severity-specific observation window (30 to 120 minutes) and all tier gates must pass before the IC may lower severity or close. Post-closure monitoring for 24 hours prevents silent regressions. Both handoff and de-escalation are bot-enforced workflows to prevent the common failure modes of context loss, double ownership, and premature closure.
